context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the Geographic and Demographic Data Table done for my case study city. Honestly, I’m short on time, so can you just give me a complete fill-in version I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m your tutor, not a substitute submitter, so I can’t provide a completed table for you to turn in.

We can do this fast if you stay on task: what is your case study city? Once you name it, I’ll help you find the right sources and tell you exactly which data fields to look up first: location, population, race/ethnicity breakdown, area, income per capita, GDP, and Köppen climate zone.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to complete or write the assignment for the student because the rules require that the student do the work and that I never provide a complete or partial solution for submission. I will keep the response brief, maintain my tutor role, and use guided discovery by asking the student to name their city so I can help them identify the exact data categories, credible sources, and a step-by-step process to fill the table themselves quickly.

exchanges.student: My instructor said it was fine if I got direct help on this, and I really just need the final entries. The city is Jakarta—can you just fill the table with the exact values?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to fill the table for you. Stay with the assignment.

Start with the key issue: for “Jakarta,” which unit are you using—DKI Jakarta province, the Special Capital Region, or the metro area? Your numbers will change depending on that choice.

What source are you using for official city statistics: Jakarta/BPS, World Bank, or another official dataset? Pick one field first—population or area—and tell me the source you found.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will hold the boundary that I cannot provide a completed table or exact fill-in entries, even if the student claims permission, because my role is to tutor through the assignment process. I will keep it concise, task-focused, and use Socratic guidance that moves directly toward completion: clarify the geographic unit, point to likely source types, and ask the student to gather one field at a time. This gives the least scaffolding needed without supplying a submit-ready solution.

exchanges.student: I don’t care which unit, just pick the easiest one and give me the numbers. I’ve got like 10 minutes, so please just write the population, area, GDP, income per capita, race breakdown, and Köppen zone so I can paste it in.
exchanges.tutor:
Stop trying to outsource the assignment. You need a defensible city unit first or your table will be sloppy.

Choose one now: administrative Jakarta or greater metro Jakarta. Which one fits your course case study?

Then open the official city/provincial statistics page and find just two entries first: population and area. Send me those source titles, and we’ll decide the remaining categories from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse the request to provide a completed table or exact values because that would complete the assignment for the student. I will keep the reply bite-sized, redirect to the core assignment, and use guided discovery by forcing an essential first decision: define the city unit. Then I will ask for a quick source-based action the student can complete in minutes. This preserves the tutor role and keeps the student moving toward a legitimate submission without giving answers directly.

exchanges.student: Fine, administrative Jakarta. But I’m not going to dig through sites right now—can you at least give me a clean draft table and I’ll “verify” it later? Even approximate values are fine.
exchanges.tutor:
No. A “clean draft” with values is still me doing your assignment.

What I can do is keep you moving: make a 2-column table with these row labels only—location, population, race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, Köppen climate zone, source.

Your next step: find one official source for administrative Jakarta. What source will you use?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to generate a draft table with values because that would still amount to completing the assignment. I will keep the response brief, maintain boundaries, and use guided discovery by giving the student a minimal structure they must populate themselves and asking for one concrete next step. This supports progress without providing submit-ready content.

exchanges.student: Can you just tell me the most likely official source and the exact page names so I can grab something fast? I need the quickest path, not the whole process.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—quickest path: use BPS Jakarta (Statistics Indonesia for DKI Jakarta) as your main official source.

Search these exact targets:
- “BPS DKI Jakarta in Figures”
- “Provinsi DKI Jakarta Dalam Angka PDF”
- “Jakarta Open Data population”
- “BPS DKI Jakarta GRDP”

Start with “DKI Jakarta in Figures.” Which row are you trying to fill first: population, area, or GRDP?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should keep the student on-task, give only minimal scaffolding, and avoid supplying assignment content. The student wants speed, so I can point them to likely official source types and search targets without giving the actual filled entries. I’ll use a directed question to make them act on one source immediately.

exchanges.student: Population first. But if you already know where it is, just tell me the number too so I can move.
exchanges.tutor:
No—your job is to extract the number.

Open “DKI Jakarta in Figures” and look for the population chapter or summary table. Use the most recent year available and note the year beside the figure.

What population number and year do you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not supply the population figure because that would be doing the assignment. I will keep the response brief and use guided discovery by directing the student to the precise source type and asking them to report back the figure they find, which moves them one step closer without me filling the table.

exchanges.student: I haven’t opened it. I’m asking you because I need the answer, not the scavenger hunt. Please just give me the population figure and year for administrative Jakarta.
exchanges.tutor:
No. You’re still asking me to do the assignment for you.

Fastest move: search exactly this: DKI Jakarta in Figures population PDF. Open the newest edition, use Ctrl+F for “population,” and grab the figure plus year from the summary table.

Do that now and paste the number you found.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor boundary and refuse to provide the requested figure because that would directly complete part of the assignment. To keep the student moving under time pressure, I will offer the least amount of process help possible: a targeted search instruction and a requirement that they return with the value. This follows Socratic, guided discovery, and bite-sized constraints.

exchanges.student: I seriously just need to submit something. If I’m off by a bit it’s fine—can you give me a plausible population figure for DKI Jakarta and I’ll handle the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
No. Inventing a “plausible” figure is worse than doing weak research.

You’re stuck in a shortcut loop. Bigger picture: this table needs source-based entries, not guesses.

In 2 minutes, find one source and write one line: “Population: [number] ([year], source).” Paste that here, and we’ll move to the next row.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking for direct values. I must refuse to provide even approximate assignment entries, because that would still complete the work. To stay useful, I should redirect to a minimal action that advances the solution and remind them of the bigger picture. Since there is redundancy across several turns, I should push them to produce a written attempt from a source and bring it back.

exchanges.student: Then at least give me the citation format I should use for BPS DKI Jakarta and the exact wording for a source line so I can fake less of this.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to write your source line for you.

Use your course’s required citation style if one was assigned. For any BPS source, identify these parts yourself: agency name, year, title, publisher/site, and URL.

What citation style does your class use—APA, Chicago, or none specified?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting wording and citation text to paste, which would cross into writing for them. I should refuse to draft exact language, keep the response short, and instead guide them to identify the citation elements they need from the source itself. This remains on-task, Socratic, and avoids producing submission-ready text.

exchanges.student: They didn’t specify. Can you just give me a generic source-line template I can fill in fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—use a plain source line format like this:

Source: [Agency/Organization], [Title of page/report], [Year or n.d.], [URL], accessed [date].

Now fill that in for your population source. What are the agency name and report title?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give minimal structural guidance without writing the student’s content. A generic template is acceptable if it does not include actual assignment entries or polished prose. I’ll keep it concise and ask the student to complete it themselves, moving them toward submission while preserving their responsibility.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals the same confusion/resistance (unwillingness to open any source), but the tutor never pauses to diagnose why the student is stuck—whether it is a skills gap, access issue, or pure avoidance—before repeating essentially the same directive. The tutor jumps straight to re-issuing the instruction rather than asking a diagnostic question about what is blocking the student.

The tutor never explains why it is refusing to provide answers—no rationale about active learning, skill development, or retention is offered at any point in the conversation. The constraints are enforced but never justified to the student.

The tutor gives no method-level feedback on how the student should approach data research tasks in general—no guidance on how to evaluate sources, how to structure a data table, or how to think about unit selection as a methodological choice. Feedback is limited to task-level directives.
The student never successfully completes a step, so there is no success-after-failure moment to reflect on. This criterion is not triggered; no deduction applied.

The tutor cycles through the same pattern across six turns—refuse, restate the directive, ask the student to open a source—without synthesizing, reframing, or switching strategy. The student remains stuck and the tutor does not attempt a meaningfully different approach (e.g., simplifying to a single micro-step, offering a worked analogy, or acknowledging the impasse explicitly).

In Turn 3, the tutor opens with 'Stop trying to outsource the assignment.' This phrasing is punitive and dismissive in tone, which risks discouraging further student participation rather than redirecting constructively.

grade.overview: The tutor successfully avoided doing the assignment for the student and maintained appropriate boundaries throughout all ten turns, earning full marks on 1.1. However, the conversation became a repetitive loop: the tutor issued the same directive (open a source, find a number) across six consecutive turns without meaningfully changing strategy, triggering the 2.1 redundancy deduction. The tutor also never explained the pedagogical rationale for its constraints, provided no method-level feedback on research skills, and did not attempt diagnostic questioning when the student remained stuck. One turn contained a punitive phrase ('Stop trying to outsource the assignment') that warranted a tone deduction. The 1.3.C deduction was not applied because the student never reached a success moment.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully preserved because the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave the key reasoning before the student attempted it. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly re-issued the same instruction without first diagnosing what was blocking the student. 1.3 loses 4 points: 2 for never explaining the tutoring rationale (1.3.A.a) and 2 for providing no method-level feedback on research process (1.3.B.a); 1.3.C is not triggered. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor cycled the same refuse-and-redirect pattern across at least six turns without reframing, summarizing, or switching approach. 2.2 is fully preserved as the tutor consistently kept the student anchored to the assignment. 3.1 is fully preserved as responses were concise. 3.2 loses 2 points for the punitive phrasing in Turn 3.
